nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and coastal reefs, including protected seaward reefs; occasionally enters estuaries. Found at depths of 1–20 m.

decorPreferences: sand substrate for burrowing at night

diet.notes: Has been documented eating small benthic invertebrates, fish eggs, mantis shrimp, and bristle worms. In captivity, tends to accept vitamin-enriched frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, chopped meaty seafood, and quality marine pellet or flake foods. Generally advised to feed 2–3 times daily given its high metabolic demands.

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 2474x1847. Tack-sharp full-frame adult showing the diagnostic vivid green body with magenta reticulation, magenta face markings, and the namesake yellow crescent (lyre) tail.
Leonard Low; 2384x1520. Crisp full-body adult with vivid green flanks, orange/green facial pattern and blue-pink pectoral fin; strong daylight coloration.
Rickard Zerpe; 2981x2236. Sharp bluer colour phase with pink radiating face lines and clear yellow crescent tail; adds phase variety.

summary: The Lunare Wrasse, also sold as the Lyretail Wrasse (Thalassoma lunare), is a large, fast-moving Indo-Pacific species widely considered one of the more aggressive wrasses kept in the aquarium hobby. Juveniles display blue and black coloration that gives way to vivid green patterning with yellow crescent markings in adults. Best suited to large fish-only systems.
careNotes: The Lunare Wrasse is also commonly sold under the trade name Lyretail Wrasse; both names refer to the same species, Thalassoma lunare. It is generally considered one of the more boisterous and aggressive wrasses available in the hobby — it tends to harass and bully tankmates, particularly new additions, and is generally best introduced last into an established aquarium. It has been known to eat mantis shrimp and bristleworms, which can be advantageous, but also tends to consume beneficial clean-up crew invertebrates such as cleaner shrimp and small crabs. A tightly fitting lid is widely considered essential, as this species has been reported to jump frequently. It tends to bury itself in sand at night; a sand bed of at least 5 cm is generally recommended. The Lunare Wrasse is not considered reef safe — it has been reported to eat small invertebrates and harass reef inhabitants, even if it does not directly nip coral tissue. Juveniles display a distinctly different blue-and-black coloration that transitions to vivid green with yellow crescent markings as the fish matures; juveniles are sometimes sold separately under their own descriptions. This species is a protogynous hermaphrodite — meaning individuals begin life as females and may transition to male.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is rarely if ever achieved under typical aquarium conditions. The Lunare Wrasse is a protogynous hermaphrodite — individuals begin life as females, and dominant fish may transition to males over time.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Labridae, Perciformes), native range (Indo-Pacific, Red Sea to Central Pacific, 1–20 m depth), wild maximum size (45 cm TL males), temperature (24–28°C), and protogynous hermaphroditism. Wild max size significantly exceeds aquarium-reported values; aquarium range of 20–30 cm used based on hobbyist sources.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (25 cm), temperature (24–28°C), pH (8.0–8.4), salinity (SG 1.020–1.025), tank size (300 L), jumping behavior, sand-burrowing at night, and protogynous nature. Temperature range averaged with LiveAquaria to yield 23–27°C.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding is rarely if ever achieved in captivity. Protogynous hermaphrodite — females transition to males under social pressure.
